desc:
This riverside forest is comparatively light, at least when one glances at the
older and thicker part north of here. Mostly birch and alder trees grow at this
particular spot, interspersed with the occasional fir and pine tree. The
undergrowth consists mainly of some light-green, high grass, along with a few
other hardier kinds of shrubs and ferns. South from here, the forest thins out
and gradually gives way to a grassy plain.
name: Mixed Forest

desc:
This would be the ideal setting for a camp, if it were not for the jagged rocks
that litter this grassy plain. North and east from here, a dark evergreen forest
obscures the view of the river Anduin. There is no denying that the river is
there though, since the noise and smell of that major waterway easily carries on
over to this spot.
name: Grassy Plain

desc:
Long blades of grass, along with many slender weeds, fill the meadow here. Some
of these grass blades are a light shade of green and very long; their ends often
curl over. Brown, drier blades of grass stand out in tufts here and there in the
meadow.
name: Grassy Meadow

desc:
Many shades of green fill the meadow between the long grasses, weeds, and other
kinds of vegetation. There is an occasional plant that is slightly brown in
colour among the green grass. Most of these brown plants though, are vaguely
noticeable because of the overwhelming amount of green throughout the meadow.
There are also a few small saplings growing on the eastern side.
name: In a Meadow

desc:
The rich soil that beds the meadow is very soft, with brightly coloured green
grass to cover it. There are only a few bushes blended among the grass of the
meadow and they are very short in height. The bushes are not very wide either.
They are adorned by many bundles of small, oval shaped leaves. The meadow
appears to continue to the south, though it begins to fade on the far eastern
edge along a riverbank.
name: Fertile Meadow

desc:
The river flows by just east of the bank here. There are several rocks, stones
and small pebbles that align the bank at the waters edge. They are all different
shades of brown, grey, black, and even a few maroon coloured ones. Some are
small, and a few of them are rather large. Each one has its own unique texture
and finish to it. The bank continues towards the south, with a meadow on the
western edge of the bank.
name: Riverbank

desc:
Large, circular and oval-shaped stones line the bank at the waters edge. The
Anduin runs swiftly in a southward flow, east of the bank. A few reeds have
sprouted on the south edge of the bank, right along the water. Grass from the
western meadow thins out as it blends into the weeds along the bank.
name: Stone Laid Riverbank

desc:
Many different coloured stones lie along the edge of the riverbank. The stones
have various shades to them, as well as different types of textures. Some of
these are brown with a rough texture, others being a shade of grey that have a
flat, smooth texture. The western meadow slowly emerges its growth, leaving a
few patches of grass along the bank.
name: Stone Filled Riverbank

desc:
The rushing water of the Great River Anduin swiftly flows southbound just east
of the riverbank. The long grass from the meadow meets the rocky riverbank, with
some iris plants of different colours mixed in between. The stones that line the
bank have a glossy finish to them, and they vary greatly in colour. The grass
from the nearby meadow to the west has grown over onto a small portion of the
bank. Several trees are visible towards the north, where a forest grows.
name: Meadow Meeting a Riverbank

desc:
This forest of great, grizzled old firs looms above the swift-flowing surface of
the Anduin. The trees are old, and their crowns are thick enough to block out
quite a lot of the sky, which probably explains the relative sparseness of
undergrowth. North and east of here one can see the surface of the mighty river
Anduin.
name: Tall Forest

desc:
The bank, as it continues further south, makes a very steep decline from here.
There are a lot of small pebbles and stones that lie atop the drier soil of the
bank. However as the bank makes its decline the soil becomes very soft, with
larger stones of an odd shape that line the bank closer to the waters edge.
Towards the south-east corner of the bank, many reeds have clustered together in
their growth. It appears the course of the river shifts along this particular
bank, as it flows south into a sharp curve leading back towards the west. The
curve of the river has caused the bank to have a rounded shape as the river
borders both the eastern and southern bank here.
name: Steep Riverbank

desc:
The riverbank is quite wide here, as if the river had at one time overcome much
of the bank. Scattered along the bank are a few stones, along with some oddly
grown patches of weeds in different locations. The bank forms a very steep
incline to both the north and west, where it appears to meet a wild meadow on
both those edges.
name: Riverbank Overflow

desc:
The bank of the river is quite treacherous, mostly due to the fact that the
steep bank is swiftly being eroded by the river. There are various kinds of
young trees growing here, quite the opposite from the other side of the river,
where some quite impressive, old oaks huddle close to the water. They seem to be
dipping their roots into the rushing waters.
name: East Bank of the Anduin River

desc:
A slight rise, at least when compared to the surrounding land, provides a neat
view of the rolling plains and forest lands of these parts of Arda. Far east of
here, a rock wall cuts through the land, obscuring the view of whatever might be
behind it. Looking westwards, a few seagulls in the sky give away the location
of the great river Anduin.
name: Plains

desc:
This area would have been ideal for a farmstead, considering the lushness of the
plain. The proximity to the great Anduin river grants these fields a gracious
amount of water, turning them into prime grazing lands. However, the land is
sorely lacking of signs of habitation. If anybody ever did live here, they have
left a long time ago.
name: Lush Plain

desc:
Here, the muddy bank of the swift river threatens to crumble and fall down into
the Anduin. This is probably due to the eroding forces of spring floods which
have long passed by, transporting away the supporting hard-packed soil. Some of
the young aspen that grow on the banks have indeed fallen over and lodged
themselves halfway into the river. The saplings are now making trails in the
flowing water, as if waiting to be torn away and transported downstream to the
Sea.
name: East Bank of the Anduin River

desc:
The grassland begins to expand towards the east from here, though along the west
it greets a riverbank. Iris plants stand mainly between the western bank and the
grass, however, there are a few that have sprouted among the long, fine blades
of grass. Several weeds grow on the northern edge of the grassland, with spiny
shaped leaves.
name: Grassland

desc:
The riverbank along the Anduin is bare here, other than the long irises that are
growing sparsely along the eastern edge of the river where it greets the
grassland. The footing upon the bank is soft from all the moisture absorbed off
the rushing water from the river. Many weeds have grown along the northern edge
of the bank here, with small leaves of dark green colour bundled closely to
their stems.
name: Riverbank

desc:
Almost the entire bank along the river is populated by the growth of dark green
iris plants. Each stem of the iris is long and slender, with many sword-shaped
leaves that have extended from the plant in every direction. The soil along the
bank is very soggy close to the river; under the irises it is very damp from
moisture, but stable enough to walk on with ease.
name: Riverbank of Irises

desc:
Tall blades of grass cover the field in assorted green colours. There are a few
stems of brown with grains at their tips; they have slumped over slightly due to
their own weight. Jagged edged leaves of a dark green colour are arrayed over
the stems of weeds here. The grassland appears to expand towards every direction
other than the west, for there lies a riverbank in the distance.
name: Expanding Field

desc:
Long, slender blades of grass consume the stretching field. Most of the grass is
a soft green colour, appearing very refined in texture. However, there are a lot
of dried up, dead blades of grass that have nestled along the base of the much
younger grass that is still in growth. The decaying blades scattered among the
ground are various tones of brown. A few weeds have sprouted themselves from the
earth, blending in well with the grass due to their similar green colour.
name: Vast Field

desc:
Wild grains of some sort stand very tall here and are scattered throughout the
grass of the field. Though the grain has risen over the blades of grass, the
grass still reaches an impressive height. Between the grains and the grass being
so thickly grown, it is very difficult to spot even the few weeds that have
hidden themselves in the field. The vegetation of different plants growing here
gives a blending mix of colours.
name: Field of Grain

desc:
Wild, brown grains are clearly dominating the grass in the field. The grain
stands very tall, though each one is quite slender. Slightly visible through the
thick, growing grain are light green blades of grass. The grass is very scant in
comparison to the wild grains. The field continues in all directions.
name: Wild Field of Grain

desc:
Grain flourishes in the field here among the grass. The grain plants are all
several feet high, with a slender stem of light brown. When the stem finally
reaches its tip, one can see the actual seeds of grain which are hanging, most
being cluttered tightly together. The grain seeds are a darker tone of brown
than the stem. A few soft green blades of grass have grown between some of the
grain.
name: Grainy Field

desc:
The field is quite thick, filled with grains and grass standing tall above the
soil. In all directions the field seems to be very expansive. There is an even
mixture of many different plants, all giving off their own natural essence to
the field. Along the ground, there are many decaying blades of grass, as well as
younger grass that has begun its growth.
name: Untamed Field

desc:
An even mixture of grain and lengthy grasses is covering most of the field.
Natural shades of green and brown from the plants in the field blend together so
well that only a few of the scrubby looking weeds stand out. The weeds have a
slight difference in tone to the grass surrounding them, making them a little
more noticeable.
name: Intermingled Field

desc:
The grass of the field here becomes very coarse and tattered looking. Each blade
of wild grass is thick, being deep green in colour, until it reaches to the tip
of the blade. Most of the blades have dried out at the very tip. There, the deep
green diminishes to a dark, browned edge. Some brown grains have sprouted up in
random areas throughout the field of grass.
name: Rough Field

desc:
A large portion of the field is covered by grass. However, there are a few spots
that reveal bare soil, edged by weeds and coarse grass. The rest of the blades
of grass, within the field, are of various colours, with differing textures.
Some of the blades are green, others a light coloured brown. There are a few
blades that look as if they are two-toned, green in the centre of the blade,
with edges of light brown.
name: Field of Grass

desc:
The thick field is composed of long, green blades of grass. Some of them are so
light that their ends curl over, dipping back towards the earth. Some dead grass
has bundled up along the base of the grass which is still in healthy growth.
There is a riverbank to the west, but the grass continues to thrive in every
other direction.
name: Thick Grassy Field

desc:
Several iris plants grow in massive bundles across the bank along the edge of
the water. Along the northern portion of the bank is where the irises seem to
really flourish. Towards the southern area on the bank, younger plants of the
iris family have emerged from the soil. They are much shorter than those that
are fully grown; their long, sword-shaped leaves are very distinctive.
name: Bank Covered with Irises

desc:
The footing along the riverbank is softened from the nearby river, but is rather
stable to travel upon. Several reeds are growing along the western side of the
bank, close to the river. Iris plants of different colours have bloomed in
several locations upon the bank, but the larger portions gathered together lie
at the east end of the bank. There, lush grass is noticeable, blending in with
the irises.
name: Riverbank

desc:
Each long, lush blade of grass here extends to a tall height above the soil. The
grass is a light shade of green, with a very refined texture. Though the grass
appears to really expand to the east, there are several iris plants growing
along the western side of the grass, where the riverbank lies. Even the footing
here appears to be very soft, evident from the great amount of dampness beneath
the soil.
name: Lush Lea of Green

desc:
The grass is extremely tall here, including the blades that cover the slight dip
that depresses towards the eastern side of the grassland. The grass blades are
very refined, being a soft coloured green. Within the dip that leads east, the
grass is rather soggy, as the rain water that has built up must have travelled
down the depression in the same frequent pattern.
name: Dip in the Grassland

desc:
The surrounding grassland has long, light coloured green blades of grass which
keep the mere more secluded. The mere itself is of an odd shape, neither exactly
in the shape of a circle, nor an oval. Instead, it almost appears to be the
shape of a crescent, with jagged edges all around the water. On the western side
there is a small indent leading into the water of the mere. The indent is
noticeable from all of the dead grass in its course. It appears that the water
has slowly trickled from the dip in the grassland in the west, forming the small
mere from all of the buildup. The water is not very clean, as all of the grass
it has drowned can be seen decaying under the water.
name: Encircled Mere

desc:
There is a slight depression in the grassland here which inclines slowly on the
eastern side. The incline is gradual, not very steep even at its highest point.
The soil of the grassland is very soft and moist. Long blades of grass that have
grown here look very unhealthy, some are even frayed upon their edges. Along the
western edge of the grassland, where the ground becomes level, lies a dark hatch
that stands out as a landmark among the surroundings.
name: Marked Grassland

desc:
Many different coloured blades of grass consume the field here. However, there
are a few bushes that have grown within the field, with many oval-shaped leaves
of dark green upon each branch. An indentation through the centre of the field
reveals that something rather large has trampled the grass. It travels in a
straight line between the east and the west.
name: Lengthy Field

desc:
The field is mixed with different coloured blades of grass. Some of the blades
are green, but the majority is a golden brown. There are a few places within the
field where the wild grass has been flattened, only to leave the once healthy
blades to decay, smashed into the soil. Two patches of bare soil are also
noticeable within the field.
name: Demolished Field

desc:
The majority of the blades of grass here are green in colour, with only a few
hints of brown at the southern portion of the grassland. Several shrubs have
grown far apart from one another, with branches filled with many dark green
coloured leaves. A slight depression can be seen in the grassland, dipping
gradually as it continues north.
name: Grassland

desc:
The grassland is mixed with assortments of grass and a few, short shrubs. The
shrubs have leaves that are very tiny, but each branch is filled with many of
these oval-shaped leaves of dark green. The grass has many different shades of
green, some even brown, with textures that vary. Some of the textures appear
very coarse, whereas others are soft and refined.
name: Grassland

desc:
The blades of grass are of different lengths, along with a variety of lush green
to their colour. All of the grass, however, appears very refined in its texture.
Some of the blades which are longer begin to curl over at their tips. Along the
southern side of the grassland, the grass becomes more blended with blades that
are a darker, green colour. Those blades have a texture that appears coarse, if
compared to the lush green blades that cover the majority of the grassland.
name: Lush Grassland

desc:
There is no sight of a single weed in growth here in the grassland. The grass
looks exceedingly fine and is not very tall. It is quite green; each blade looks
as healthy as it could possibly be. In the east, the grassland appears to
expand, more wild in growth than it is here. Along the western side of the
grassland, irises and reeds from the nearby bank greet each other, blending
themselves into the grass.
name: Lush Lea of Green

desc:
Once long, slender reeds grew tall along the bank; now they lie smashed into the
soft soil. A few irises bloom along the eastern edge of the bank, where it has
begun to blend with the tall blades of lush grass. There are several tiny rocks
that have been clustered together along the western side of the bank. The rocks
are all dark in colour, but vary in shape.
name: Flattened Riverbank

desc:
The riverbank has many reeds lying loosely upon the soil. Towards the northern
portion of the bank, there are several that are still rooted in. These appear to
stand very tall and still even in strong winds. On the edge of the bank, closer
to the river, the reeds have been trampled into the ground so much that they
look as though they were just a part of the muddy bank.
name: Trampled Riverbank of Reeds

desc:
There is a large combination of thorny brush and long blades of grass along the
south here. Each thorny stem of the brush is long and has a tangled appearance
to it. The many brown and thin stems are greeted by coarse blades of grass at
soil level. To the north are blades of grass that are a darker green and not as
rough looking in their texture.
name: Mixed Ground

desc:
There is a vast mixture of vegetation growing along the ground here. Lush, green
blades of grass are growing thickly together towards the north. A few weeds have
begun to grow along the eastern edge, blended in among the different lengths of
the grass. Towards the southern edge of the grassland, the brush becomes
visible, though it is very thin. Throughout the grassland, there are various
shaded blades of grass, of all different lengths. Their textures seem to vary
from being very coarse in appearance, to very refined.
name: Mixed Ground

desc:
The grassland has long blades of green grass that have grown thick here, all
having a fine texture to them. The grass begins to blend towards the east into
many different shades of green, some even brown, as it becomes a little more
thickly grown. Southwards, some brush is visible and the ground looks very bare,
with only a few, small rocks atop the soil.
name: Grassland

desc:
Along the northern portion of the grassland the grass is the more lush in
appearance, and more thickly grown as well. Towards the far southern edge of the
grassland, many stones lie upon the bare soil and it appears that the grass is
slowly coming to its end. The coarse grass that has grown in the southern area
of the grassland is very dry and brown in colour. Several weeds have sprouted in
random locations throughout the grassland and there are some large stones
embedded in the grass.
name: Blended Grassland

desc:
The field has an overall golden-brown hue to it, since every length of grass is
of that colour. The blades are all very long and slender, yet refined in
texture. The soil has been revealed from under the grass in a few locations,
though even that is a light shade of brown, blending into the colour of the
grass. Towards the south, a very bare plain can be noticed.
name: Golden-hued Field

desc:
Many rocks, stones and tiny pebbles of different shades of brown, grey and black
lie on the plain. They all have their own unique shape. Some of the rocks have a
rough texture to them and others are quite smooth. There are very few patches of
grass growing in this part of the plain, due to all the rocks which lie
scattered about, smothering the soil. The few grass patches that do exist,
appear to be very coarse; for the grass, there is a great struggle to stay alive
here, much less to thrive in growth. Towards the north of the plain, the
grassland has started. Its light, golden blades of grass meet the dry soil
there. The bare plain continues along to the east of it.
name: Rocky Plain

desc:
There are many large patches of rocky soil in the plain here. As it continues to
the north, the soil becomes hidden by lush grass. There are a few larger rocks
that the grass has encompassed, but most of the rocks sit in the dirt free from
grass. A few weeds also grow in the plain. The weeds have sprouted only where
the rocks have hindered the grass from growth; on the northern side of the plain
where there is more grass, there is not a single weed in sight. The brush from
the west is slowly beginning to expand into the plain here.
name: Bare Plain

desc:
Scrawny, low growing stems that emerge from the bare soil are struggling to grow
here. To the west the brush seems to be much more plentiful than it is in this
area. A few pebbles and stones appear on the eastern edge of the thin brush,
with soil so bare that one can see even the smallest weed trying to grow. As the
brush thickens towards the south, many dark coloured thorns on each stem are far
more visible. Northward, in the distance, is an expanding grassland.
name: Diffused Brush

desc:
Green grass from the northern meadow meets the reclining brush. As the brush
continues south, it becomes exceedingly dense in its growth. On the many stems
that form the brush, there are tiny thorns attached. There are various kinds of
weeds as well, in many different shapes, sizes, and colours that blend together
well. Eastward, the brush really begins to thin out as it forms into a bare
plain.
name: Reclined Brush

desc:
The brushwood forms along the edge of a thinly wooded forest to the west. Many
bushes and shrubs have spread wide apart from one another throughout the vast
brushwood that expands on its way into the east. Tightly bundled stems of brown
are reaching out from the soil, creating the majority of the vegetation here.
Broken-off branches from the nearby forest lie tangled among the brushwood. A
few of the weeds growing here have prickly covered leaves of a dark green hue.
To the south it appears that the brush is becoming more thick and tangled.
name: Brushwood

desc:
Slender trees stand here with long, reaching branches and large, green leaves.
Though the forms of these trees are slender, they still reach to a tall height
above. Many immature trees are spread out across the ground of the scant forest.
Several vast stumps are still rooted into the earth, remaining of old trees cut
down quite some time ago. Some trees that were cut down more recently lie on the
ground with their branches still intact.
name: Scant Forest

desc:
In the north, the river makes an S-shaped curve before returning to its original
course towards the south. The bank is surrounded by the water on its northern
and western side, trees of the forest loom above in the south, and a thinly
wooded forest spreads towards the east. Several reeds have grown up from the
soggy earth along the rushing waters off the bank of the Anduin. There are also
several stones, of many different sizes and colours, aligning the bank at the
waters edge. Cornered by the trees to the far southeast corner of the bank, lies
a fire pit made of large stones. There are several pieces of debris from the
nearby forest lying upon the riverbank. A large piece of canvas has been left
here, along with the wooden poles that once held it up.
name: Deserted Camp

desc:
Coarse grass is growing in bundles throughout the plain. There is a large area
where the bare soil is revealed with nothing growing in its vicinity. Two large
rocks have settled deep into the earth in the midst of the plain. They each have
a jagged texture, though they are also matching in a light brown colour. A few
weeds have grown up around the massive rocks.
name: Plain of Rocks

desc:
The brush slowly begins to fade here from the thick, brown plants that grow in
the west and south. There are several rocks on the ground, where the soil looks
very bare and dry. A few weeds growing here are dark green in their colour, with
many leaves sprouted from each stem. Fine, tiny hairs grow upon each leaf of the
weeds in the brush. The brush itself has several thorns that are spread apart on
each long, tangled stem.
name: Fading Brush

desc:
Quite a few pebbles, some larger stones, and clumps of soil lie across the dry
earth. Some tangled debris also remains scattered around the bare plain. From
the looks of the debris, it appears to have come from some kind of brush plants.
A few thorns are noticeable upon the tangled stems of the debris. Some brush can
be seen to the west, giving a brown hue to that direction.
name: Bare Plain near the Brush

desc:
Much of the soil is exposed on this plain. There are only a few patches of crisp
looking grass, scattered far apart from one another. These patches of grass only
show green at the base of each blade where it continues downward into the earth.
As each blade gets taller though, the green begins to fade into brown. A few
rocks, and clumps of loose soil, are noticeable on the eastern side of the
plain.
name: Barren Plain

desc:
The diminishing brush appears to come to an end here from the larger portion to
the north and west. Towards the east, the footing becomes extremely barren, with
various stones lying upon it. Several weeds have grown in between the thorny
stems of the brush, their hue of green making them very noticeable among all of
the brown colours. The weeds have tiny hairs covering their leaves, all of them
being very short and fine.
name: Diminished Brush

desc:
The deeper into the brush one goes, the thicker it becomes. The brown, thorny
stems reach to waist height for most. The soil here is very dry, with a few
weeds of dark green colour, that have emerged from the earth and grown to a
decent height. The stems in the brush have tangled themselves around a few of
the trees that have been growing on the western edge. Even further towards the
west, is a large amount of trees emerging from the nearby forest.
name: Dense Brush

desc:
Surrounding the forest to the east, north, and west are scattered brush and some
brambles. Though there are several different types of trees in the forest here,
it is not dense at all. The lush green grass beds the spaces between the trees
at decent lengths. Each tree has a different coloured leaf and bark than the
next one does. The barks of the trees are not only arrayed in several different
shades of brown, but their textures vary as well.
name: Thin Forest

desc:
Thorny bushes and large weeds have grown thickly together to form the brushwood
between the trees of the forest. There are quite a few slender, short trees in
growth mixed between some of the brush. Most of the brush is leafed in a dark
green hue, but the larger, thorny vegetation in the brush is brown. The thorns
on the brush appear to be small, but rather sharp at the tip. On both of the
eastern and western sides of the brush, the trees of the nearby forest begin to
expand further into the brush.
name: Conjoined Brushwood

desc:
The brush expands in every direction from here, other than where the forest has
begun to unfold to the south. The brush is dense from both the thickly grown
stems bundled together, and from thorns that have extended from their branches,
so that it would be quite difficult to pass through to the north or east. Many
large, brown patches of coarse grass grow among the vegetation in the brush.
There are weeds that have fine hairs spread about over each leaf, with a few
sharp thorns growing off of their stems. The weeds are very tall, even though
their girth is not very wide at all. Many of these weeds line themselves along
the northern portion of the brush.
name: Teeming Brush

desc:
The brush has a tangled appearance to it. Vegetation from the forest to the west
has expanded greatly into the dry, thorny brush. Several weeds that are growing
here stand out alongside the other plants because of their rather large leaves.
Brown, dried up blades of grass stand tall, scattered within the brush in
thickly grown patches.
name: Tangled Brush

desc:
The trees of the forest have grown to a great height here. The leaves are soft
and mint coloured, which contrasts with the light brown bark that protects the
trunks of these trees. Strands of ivy hang in several of the trees. The ivy has
climbed even the highest of branches, disappearing beyond sight. The leaves on
each strand of ivy are a dark green, with slight hints of white which extend
throughout each leaf from the centre.
name: Ivy-draped Forest

desc:
Trees of various heights are growing in the forest here, broadly spaced from one
another. There are a few different types of trees as well. Some look as though a
darker green leaf grows from the branches, others are a lighter shade of green.
A few strands of ivy have laced their way up the trunks of a few, select trees.
Long, refined grass is edged along the very base of some of the trunks, but as
it expands from around the trunk it gets shorter in length. Westwards a
riverbank is visible in the distance.
name: Forest along the Riverbank

desc:
Many long stems of green and brown reeds are clustered together along the muddy
riverbank, whereas the trees of a forest line the bank along its eastern edge.
The Great River runs swiftly to the south. Spread out between the reeds are
delicate looking plants that belong to the iris family. The ground is very soft,
due to the amount of moisture brought to the area from the river.
name: Riverbank of Reeds

desc:
Along the east side of the Great River Anduin a gentle sloped riverbank greets a
nearby forest. There are many thick reeds covering the sloped bank. A few iris
plants are lining the northeastern edge of the thick forest. The footing is
stable, but the ground becomes rather moist and soft as it merges into the
waters of the river.
name: Sloped Riverbank

desc:
Ivy scales up the trunks of the trees in delicate, tiny strands. Their leaves
are evergreen, with a glossy finish to each. At a closer glance, tiny white
veins are revealed, as they scatter out of the centre of each leaf. The
undergrowth in the forest is not very dense. Many trees have grown close
together however, making travelling through the forest somewhat difficult.
name: Ivy-draped Forest

desc:
Long, slender stems rise several feet above the ground. The reeds are in various
shades of green, some even lightly browned. The rushing water of the Great River
flows along the riverbank on both its northern and western edge. Nearby to the
east stands a forest with large trees. Under the eaves of the trees on the
eastern edge of the bank grow plants that appear to be irises. The footing is
soft from all of the moisture the river produces. A few rocks are scattered
along the northern border of the bank where the river has washed them ashore.
name: Riverbank of Reeds

desc:
The forest is thinly wooded, with lush, long blades of green grass bedding the
soil between the trees. A few healthy bushes grow beneath the trees, each one
filled with many oval shaped leaves. On the western edge of the forest, a couple
of reeds are visible from the nearby riverbank. On the northern portion of the
forest, there are even more reeds gathered together. Some have been mixed within
some extremely long blades of grass. A few iris plants seem to flourish in
growth here near the banks.
name: Forest along the Riverbank

desc:
There are several stems growing out of the ground at the base of each shrub here
in the brush. Most of the stems are very slender, almost bare of leaves, giving
the brush an overall woody appearance. A few coarse, lightly browned patches of
grass have sprouted within the brush as well. The brush gets more intense as it
unfolds into the east. In the distant south, and north, the brush continues but
a vast forest emerges and greets the western front.
name: Unfolding Brush

desc:
Many of the trees in the forest have grown close together here. There are
several low branches expanding out from even the most slender of the trees
present. Dark green, stout bushes are also adding to the already dense forest.
Long blades of grass can be seen growing from the dry and coarse undergrowth.
name: Crowded Forest

desc:
The brush is very dense and difficult to manoeuvre through. There seems to be a
slight indentation noticeable through the brush, as if some form of being had
passed through before. Even with the slightly cleared path, the brush consists
of many thorny, rugged bushes, causing each movement to be quite complicated.
Passage only appears possible towards either the east or west through the brush.
To continue north or south is impossible due to the thickness of the bushes and
their many sharp thorns.
name: Dense Brushwood

desc:
There is a tiny depression in the middle of the brush that indicates travel has
been done before through the thorny plants. The thorns on these bushes are not
small, but rather large, making their sharpness all the more perceptible.
Attempting to move through the brush in a northward direction, or to the east,
is rather impossible due to the thickness of the brush.
name: Dense Brush

desc:
Through the dense northern and eastern brush, a rift has formed. The thorny
brush is impenetrable to the south and the west. The soil of the rift itself is
very barren, causing a lot of loose particles of dry dirt to flit around in the
air once it has been disturbed. Along the edges of the rift, there are many
weeds growing under the thorny bushes. Though they are only some simple weeds,
their various shades of green add some colour to the surrounding brown, dense
brush.
name: Rift through the Brush

desc:
Vast thorns grow on the bushes with fine, sharpened edges to each thorn. The
branches are dark brown, the texture of each one alludes to the extreme dryness
that affects the area. A slight gap between some of the brush hints that some
kind of travel has gone through here before. The gap between the brush is very
narrow, only leading east or west. The brush is so dense towards the north and
south, that passage in either direction would impossible.
name: Thick Brush

desc:
Creating a great 'S', the wild river Anduin cuts a broad path through the
landscape. The current continues southwards, while upstream, the river bends
westwards. Trees line the shores on both sides; however, the growth is far too
sparse to be described as proper forest.
name: Bend in the Great River Anduin

desc:
Coming out of an S-shaped bend, the mighty Anduin flows towards the sea in a
straight, southward direction. Along the western shore, a tall forest graces the
riverside, while to the east a rolling plain stretches out for miles and miles.
Upstream, the river appears to bend around a patch of forest.
name: The Anduin

desc:
The river flows in a southbound direction here. Though the water flows very
fast, it is still clear enough to see the bottom of the river. There are many
pebbles beneath the rushing water, lying along the riverbed. To the west, a
nearby meadow can be seen meeting the riverbank. There is grass visible on the
eastern bank as well.
name: Anduin River

desc:
The rushing water flows southbound, between a riverbank of irises on the east,
and a bank of many stones on the west. The water flows quickly, which makes it
harder to view the bottom of the river. Through the ripples, however, several
stones are visible. The stones are rather large, though they do not stand over
the surface of the water.
name: Anduin River

desc:
As the river flows south, the water looks as though it accelerates. There are
several stones lining the bottom of the river, visible through the ripples of
the clear water. Some of the stones are large, others quite small in size, but
each noticeable from their dark colour. Along the eastern riverbank there are
many iris plants growing. To the west, there are many large stones resting on
the riverbank.
name: Anduin River

desc:
The water of the Great River is very clear, though it is harder to see the
bottom of the river through the ripples from the fast flowage. There are some
wide shaped stones that are visible through the water, noticeable from their
dark colours causing them to stand out. Irises are growing along the eastern
bank of the river, very close to the waters edge.
name: Anduin River

desc:
The western bank of the mighty Anduin, which appears to be particularly steep,
leads to a grassy meadow that lies above. The water of the river is rather
clear, with a very chilling appearance to it. The bottom of the river is very
hard to view clearly, not from dirty waters, but rather from the quick flow of
the river as it drives south at a fast speed. Towards the east, along the bank,
the ground seems to have been disrupted by some means, as the soil has been
piled up in one particular place, close to the rushing waters edge.
name: Anduin River

desc:
The river travels from the north until it plunges into the southern bank,
turning towards the west as it continues in the river bend. Along the southern
bank, bare soil with a lot of debris can be noticed. The eastern bank has many
reeds, but most appear to have been trampled. The soil that lies along the
eastern and southern edges of the river has been dug up. The water of the river
is very clear, though it is chilly in its temperature.
name: Bend in the Anduin River

desc:
The river comes from the northern direction, bending towards the west again as
the water continues its steady flow. Along the eastern bank, the soil appears
quite level with the river. The bank that lies to the east has many visible
pieces of debris. On the edge of the southern bank, many reeds are flourishing
near the clear and chilly water. The water becomes slightly clouded over here
due to the rushing flow that breaks away the soil at the bank.
name: Bend in the Anduin River

desc:
Flowing steadily to the south into what appears to be a bend, the mighty river
Anduin broadens slightly; thus allowing rich beds of ferns to grow high along
the river banks. Some unevenness or another on the bottom of the river causes
quite a lot of eddies and swirls to appear mid-river. A lot of debris,
presumably from the forests south and west of here, seems to have been caught in
the whirlpools.
name: Bend in the Great River Anduin

desc:
The Great River flows from the east, running along the curved edge of the bank
on its northwestern side, and continues to flow southbound from here. There are
a lot of pebbles and stones which are visible along the bottom of the river. The
bank to the north appears to be very steep from this distance, though towards
the west it looks flatter.
name: Bend in the Anduin River

desc:
The river has travelled from the east here, taking a bend towards the south as
it continues its course in that direction. Towards the northern bank, it is
noticeable how level the soil is, appearing as though at one time long ago the
river had even flowed over the bank there. The river itself expands a wide
distance from each bank. The water does not flow as fast here, but it is very
clear to see the riverbed it flows upon, through the ripples of the water.
name: Anduin River

desc:
Many blackberry bushes cover the ground making it difficult to travel. To the
west the terrain descends to the river, while to the east and south vast plains
can be seen. Small tracks can be seen all around on the ground, revealing
intense activity of insects and small animals.
name: Thick Bush

desc:
This vast grassy plain extends in all directions. No sign of any kind of
humanoid activity can be seen. To the west, towards the river, the vegetation
becomes thicker and hard to cross. To the east the steep banks of a hill block
the way.
name: In the Plains

desc:
The ground is dry and bare, and occasional cracks and fissures make travelling
through this area quite dangerous. No vegetation but grass and heather grows
here. The terrain becomes harder to travel to the west, approaching the river.
To the east and south lies a grassy plain, while to the north the ground is
dotted with rocks mixed with grass.
name: Cracked Ground

desc:
The grassy plains extend towards the north, east and south from these mixed
plains. To the west the terrain is quite rocky and becomes difficult to travel
across while riding a mount. Some trees can be seen growing in this part of the
plains. A little track made by animals leads west to the river.
name: Mixed Ground

desc:
The ground is grassy and level. Some trees, surrounded by a number of rocks and
boulders, grow among the grass giving shelter to birds and little animals. To
the north and east some woody slopes can be seen, while to the south the plains
seem to continue for miles.
name: Plains between the River and Forest

desc:
The grass is lush and thick in this area, probably due to the influence of the
Great River Anduin that flows nearby to the west. To the east and north, lines
of trees mark the beginning of a forest. A vast plain continues to the south. A
little animal track comes from the forest to the east.
name: Plains near a Forest

desc:
This patch of ground dips below the surrounding plains. The borders of the
depression resemble the shape of a bowl. On the eastern slope some shelves of
sand and rock border a small crack, just large enough to fit through.
name: Sunken Patch of Ground

desc:
To the larger creatures of Arda the height of the grass might be a mere
hindrance. However, to a hobbit, dwarf and to other smaller creatures the height
of the grass holds a real danger of getting lost as the tall stalks go on
forever as an entangled maze. One can only pick a direction and hope that it
leads out of the sea of grass.
name: Tall Grass

desc:
To the north and west the ruins of a stone wall can be seen, but this particular
area is virtually devoid of anything but grass. If it were not for the
occasional landmarks of small trees and stones, it would be quite possible to
get lost in this endless sea of vegetation.
name: Tall Grass

desc:
This wall section has survived over the countless years. It is a rather
unremarkable wall, having nothing to show that it was used for any type of
military fortification in the past. In fact, it seems likely that it was used in
an attempt to keep livestock from wandering too far. This section blocks the
movement to the west and the north from here as it makes its turn to the north,
further east.
name: Sturdy Section of a Wall

desc:
A few young trees have grown up in the shade of a decaying stone wall, sheltered
from the elements. The grass is a little bit shorter having been deprived of
sunlight, giving other plants a chance to flourish. At the base of the ratty and
old stone wall, some of the stones from which it was constructed, sit scattered
around.
name: Budding Plains

desc:
The ground is grassy and covered by small plants growing in thick groups. The
terrain is slightly sloping and pleasant to travel. The plain extends to all
directions without an interruption but to the west, where a steep hillock rises
above the plains.
name: Grassy Slope

desc:
Lush grass grows all around. Many holes of prairie dogs are visible among the
vegetation. Far to the north, the horizon line is shaped by the edge of a
forest. The way south is blocked by a cliff, while the plain continues in all
other directions.
name: In the Plains

desc:
Travel across these plains is easy and pleasant. Some excrements mark the
presence of cattle and other large animals. To the north-east a forest breaks
the monotony of the landscape that continues unaltered in all other directions.
name: In the Plains

desc:
A lean-to shelter has been built against the side of a fallen tree. Whoever made
this shelter must have been in a hurry, as the result is a somewhat rickety
construction, which could have been a decent shelter had more time been spent
with it. Before the shed, some stones have been arranged in a rough circle
around the campfire's location, and old cooking pots and other trash can be seen
around, half-buried by dirt showing that this place has been abandoned for a
long time. Barely noticeable against the trunk of the fallen tree are some
rather large spots of old, dried blood.
name: At an Old Campsite

desc:
Saplings of several different types grow here in the grass. Larger trees grow up
just beyond the plains on the western side, giving way to a true forest. Perhaps
someday soon this small piece of land will cease to be a plain anymore. Some ivy
has grown along a stone wall to the south.
name: Sparse Trees

desc:
A section of an ancient wall has not withstood the test of time. Fallen stones
lay all about, partially sunken into the earth at the foot of the structure.
Moss has begun to grow on part of the wall that still stands. A path is worn
between the standing sections showing use by different creatures to come and go
as they please.
name: Break in a Wall

desc:
Standing alone, tall and forlorn, is a single dead tree. Its branches have been
stripped away by harsh weather. Over time part of the trunk has split off to
rest beside its standing portion. To the west, past the plains, the remains of a
stone wall can be seen.
name: By a Dead Tree

desc:
The native grasses have made themselves quite at home around an ancient stone
wall. The wall itself, however, seems to have seen better days. Stones are
missing along the top and side, leaving small niches for dirt and water to
collect. A few plants have taken advantage of this, growing out of the ancient
structure itself.
name: By a Crumbling Wall

desc:
The light forest gives way to larger trees here. Vines and moss can also be
seen, adding life to the branches on the forest floor. Deeper inside forest, the
songs of birds can be heard along with the occasional scurrying of a small
animal. The crumbling remains of a stone wall block any passage to the east.
name: Light Forest

desc:
Wild flowers have grown tall between the trees here, shedding their colour into
this fairly dry land. The flowers and other foliage here seem to be the perfect
habitat for insects and small animals.
name: Flowering Forest

desc:
Scattered trees mark the border of the forest that extends north and east of
here. To the west and to the south lies a vast grassy plain. Signs of animal
life are visible everywhere.
name: Some Trees

desc:
The vast field covered by lush grass and wild flowers extends to all directions,
although further to the east, the horizon is outlined by the shape of a forest.
Towards the river to the west the terrain becomes rocky and more inhospitable.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Old, dry bones litter the ground and have killed some of the grass around them,
leaving small, bare spots in the plains. Around the mound of gnawed bones some
pawprints, new and old, can be seen leading off to different directions.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
Debris from the forest just to the west is prevalent here, adding some variety
to the characteristics of a typical grass field: large branches emerge above the
grassline, twisting among the briars and weeds. To the south, a crumbling stone
wall cuts across the plains.
name: Brushy Plains

desc:
Thornbushes have grown tall and wild among the trees here, bearing long and
dangerous thorns. On some of the bushes grow different types of berries, but
reaching for them might greet the unwary scavenger with a hand full of puncture
wounds. The bushes are not too dense towards the east, south or west but to the
north they form an impenetrable brush wall, not allowing anyone or anything to
move through.
name: Thorny Patch of Woods

desc:
Oak, chestnut and elder trees grow and flourish all around. Some small rodent
holes can be distinguished among the roots, and next to the trees there is one
bigger hole as well, with several fresh footprints around it. The forest extends
to the north and east from here. Some grassy plains with scattered trees can be
seen south and west.
name: Outskirts of a Forest

desc:
The tree that was once likely the king of these woods appears to have swayed for
its last time and crashed to the ground. It is now overgrown with ivy while ants
make their nests under its bark and squirrels store their nuts in holes under
its decaying trunk. In its place, younger saplings have begun to grow in the
freed up sunlight.
name: At a Fallen Tree

desc:
There is a surprisingly wide, open area inside this tree. Long vines grow along
the edges of the trunk, and several narrow cracks are visible among them,
allowing daylight to shine upon the vegetation. The air smells of rotten wood
and mould. Mushrooms and toadstools can be seen growing out of the dead tree,
while a few skeletons of small animals lie around, accented by a deer's skull.
name: Hollow Tree

desc:
This cavern has been dug from the ground by some kind of animal. The floor is
covered by dry leaves and the walls are of bare earth with roots hanging out.
Many recent footprints show that the den is still in use.
name: Animals' Den

desc:
The great river Anduin flows close to the west. The shore is low and sandy.
Small channels in the ground lead from the eastern plain into the river; they
are bordered by lush, green grass within the plains, and laid bare while cutting
across the sandy shore to the river. Some rocks and boulders lie along the
shoreline; to the east they are replaced by grass and small plants. To the south
the terrain becomes rocky and harder to travel.
name: Bank of the River

desc:
Travelling this terrain is arduous and riding is quite impossible. Many jagged
rocks cover the ground and some huge boulders make difficult to keep a straight
direction. Some cracks line the ground, descending towards the river. To the
north and south the landscape is all the same. To the east the rocks are
replaced by grassy plains dotted with trees.
name: Rocky Ground

desc:
Approaching the river the ground becomes rocky and hard to travel, making it
impossible to ride here. To the east the terrain becomes low and grassy, while
to the south some thick vegetation can be seen. To the north the rocks and
boulders continue without a break.
name: Rocky Ground

desc:
This rocky spur dominates the river flowing below to the west. The cliff is made
of grey stone eroded by the power of the river. Some large, flat boulders with
jagged edges make moving along the riverbank a dangerous task. To the east the
cliff lowers towards a plain. The bank continues to the south. Heading west will
result in a dive into the river and climbing back onto the riverside will not be
possible.
name: Low Cliff above the River

desc:
The great power of the Anduin has carved the ground, revealing its bones. The
riverbank is made of bare rocks and only a little vegetation grows among them.
The rocks continue to the north and east, while to the south the bank becomes a
cliff.
name: Rocky Riverbank

desc:
The riverside here is low and sandy. Many smooth pebbles lie on the beach; some
are white or grey and some are of bright colours. Part of the bank is covered by
a reed thicket. To the west flows the great river Anduin. To the north the bank
becomes high and rocky. East and south the ground is covered by thick vegetation
and trees.
name: Bank of the River

desc:
Once this must have been the bed of an underground tributary of Anduin. The
walls are smooth and the ground is covered by some rocks and debris. From the
ceiling, stalactites point menacingly at the floor. Some piled boulders lead
towards a possible exit from the vault of the cavern. To the east the tunnel
narrows in the darkness.
name: Under the Cliff

desc:
The cavern here is damp and the ceiling is low. The floor is covered by debris
and it is quite clear that a stream once flowed across it. To the east, a pile
of fallen rocks make it impossible to go further. To the west, the cavern
broadens.
name: In a Cavern

desc:
Harsh, horizontal lines deface the black rock walls of this crudely cut tunnel,
making them jagged and unpredictable. Many small pebbles are strewn about the
floor, slowly dissipating as the tunnel continues to the north.
name: Small Tunnel

desc:
The tunnel looks like it would have ended here, if it was not for a crudely cut
passage leading to the south. The predominately smooth floor is a sharp contrast
to the jagged walls and ceiling, which add many hazards to those who may
traverse through.
name: Bend in a Tunnel

desc:
The rock that makes up the jagged walls of this tunnel is dark, causing the
walls to absorb any light that may be cast upon them. The floor of the tunnel is
quite rough and very dirty. Many small stalagmites are protruding every few
feet, while most of them have been shattered and broken. The signs, and the
smells, indicate trolls have made their lair here. The tunnel continues to the
east and ends at a massive pile of rubble in the west.
name: Dark Tunnel

desc:
A sloped hillock breaks up the landscape here. No tree can be seen for miles.
Only green lush grass covers the ground. The steep banks of this hillock prevent
the travelling in all directions but down.
name: Grassy Hillock

desc:
From atop this hill, one can look down and see the Anduin flowing directly to
the east, and follow it around the hill towards the south where it continues
into the horizon. The river is unreachable from here, as the banks are very
steep. A small opening which leads down into the rock can be seen on the ground,
marked by a obviously placed white stone.
name: Small Hill Overlooking the Anduin

desc:
This small valley is lower than all of the plains that surround it. It is quite
rocky, with no plant life trying to grow among the small rocks which have been
chipped off of the walls of the valley. Due to the steepness of the northern and
western walls, travelling in those directions is impossible.
name: Rocky Valley

desc:
The plains here are thriving with grass, creating a seemingly endless carpet of
green that turns to blue towards the north where the plain meets the tributary.
The plain gently rises towards the east and south, leading to the higher ground
that borders the Great River.
name: Green Plains near a Tributary

desc:
Scattered trees and brush dot the plains here, giving the impression that the
loose soil here is very fertile. Short grasses also spread across the plains,
connecting the various trees and shrubs together and giving a unified look to
the landscape.
name: Sparsely Wooded Plains

desc:
If one turns in a complete circle here, the landscape differs in each direction.
Towards the east, the river Anduin slides through the fields and plains. A tiny
wooded area can be seen toward the south, and the plain slants upwards to the
north towards what seems to be a small hill.
name: Open Plains

desc:
All around, this plain is dominated by a thick, tall grass that stands about a
yard high. While the field seems to be flat, the thick stalks and pointed tips
of the grass hide the ground from view, suggesting that it could rise and fall
slightly.
name: Tall Grass

desc:
This plain looks like many others, save for a huge blackened oak tree which
looms over the field with an ominous presence. It was probably struck by a
lightning long ago, so charred and burned are its once lively branches and
trunk.
name: At a Scorched Tree

desc:
Hard ground and dead grasses make up this barren field, giving it a solitary
look compared to the fertile plains one might expect to find here. It stands a
little bit higher than the surrounding fields. Perhaps the elevation is a factor
to this field's demise, letting water drain off into the lower meadows.
name: Barren Landscape

desc:
This hill is blanketed in short, dense grass. From atop this hill, one can see
water to the north, east, and south, as the tributary meets the Anduin, which
continues to flow southward through a small gorge. The riverbank to the east is
quite steep, making it impossible to enter the Anduin from this hill. South of
the hill, the small gorge compresses the Anduin causing it to crash through the
formation.
name: Grassy Hill Overlooking the Anduin

desc:
The field here is covered in the vast grassy carpet which flows in from all
directions and continues onward through the fields and plains giving the
impression of a seemingly endless landscape. The grassy cover slopes upwards to
the south, meeting the horizon.
name: Sloping Grasslands

desc:
This plain is all but flat, with small hills and depressions making up the
field. Short wide-bladed grass covers the whole plain, and rolls over the hills
and through each dip, never breaking to show the plain ground that hides under
the undulating grass.
name: Grassy Plain

desc:
Healthy bushes make up most of this plain, standing about half a yard in height,
and flanked by short grasses on all sides. The bushes themselves have wide,
dense leaves that shroud most of their thorny stems from eyesight. There are
also a few shorter bushes with long, pale leaves that grow sparingly around the
plain.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
Desolate and barren, this field is populated very sparsely only by patches of
grass and tiny shrubs which are struggling to maintain themselves. The brown
earth has dried with large cracks on it, likely from the lack of plants to
shield it from the strong rays of sunlight.
name: Blotted Field

desc:
Wide plains come into view here, in a vast field with rolling grasses and a few
bushes making up the scenery. The field slants down towards the east, opening up
a panorama of the various landscapes that creep toward the Anduin river in that
direction.
name: Open Field

desc:
The fields here are covered in small shrubs and brush, differing in height from
about half a yard to over three yards tall, colouring the landscape with hues of
green and brown. Towards the east, a small forest rises out of the plains,
perhaps a sign of that an abundant water source is near.
name: Thriving Plains

desc:
The plains here meet a small, sparse forest which stretches lightly to the west.
The forest seems to be made of mostly pine trees. Their bark and needles bring
new textures and colours, which contrast the grassy landscape that surrounds the
trees.
name: A Small, Sparse Forest

desc:
Sloping in from every direction, this tiny valley appears to be very fertile,
with a carpet of thick-bladed grass and many small shrubs dotting the fields.
The soil appears to be a little loose, probably from the rainwater that drains
into the field from the surrounding plains.
name: Dip in the Plains

desc:
Short, thick grass covers the land here, stretching out in all directions but
the north, where a swift tributary flows through the plains. The proximity of
the tributary has helped the grass flourish here, keeping the soil very fertile
during dry weather.
name: Rolling Grasslands near a Tributary

desc:
Green grasslands stretch out in all directions, sloping upward to the north, and
down towards the south and east. The whole field is covered in thick grass which
crawls about the landscape, giving it a soft, fertile appearance.
name: Sloping Plains

desc:
This small hill stands proudly over the tributary that runs from the cold Misty
Mountains. It commands a view in all directions. To the north, the tributary
from the Misty Mountains flows down to meet the Great River. The Anduin itself,
is in the far east, a winding ribbon heading south. Plains roll on south from
the hill, interrupted by a small, sparse forest before continuing on.
name: A Vantage Point

desc:
Grasses, thin brush, and small rocks are scattered about here, bringing a sight
that is different than the surrounding plains. Many different colours make up
this field, from the brown soil with green grasses to the grey and white rocks
that have been seemingly tossed about.
name: Mixed Ground

desc:
The plain slopes downward towards the east, opening up the view of the lands
that lead in the direction of the River Anduin. Scattered among these fields, a
few small groups of pine trees offer contrast to the grasses with their rough
bark.
name: Rich Fields

desc:
Vegetation flourishes here, with multiple small bushes and shrubs springing from
the ground, along with a short, dense grass that grows all over the plain,
seemingly endless as it connects the plants and hides the dirt from view.
name: Verdant Plains

desc:
The wide open plains bring their lush carpet of grasses here, adding to the
colours set by the small group of willow trees towards the west side of this
field. The towering willow trees appear to be well nourished, with thick foliage
hanging down from high branches, creating a canopy that shrouds their huge
roots.
name: Small Group of Trees amid the Soft Plains

desc:
The ground here is quite soft, with thick grasses and scattered brush thriving
due to the pond sitting in the north west corner of this meadow. The grass is
broken and trampled upon in many places, leading to and from the pond, a sign
that small animals converge here to replenish themselves with the water.
name: Spongy Meadow

desc:
Rich grass seems to crawl over this wide plain, hiding the ground beneath it
from eyesight. It is quite flat, with a slight upward slope towards the east.
The plain must be very fertile, as the grasses cover the landscape, save for a
few small bushes that have also found the fertile soil of this plain to be
homely.
name: Soft Plains

desc:
Lush green grasses cover this plain, along with the occasional tiny shrub. The
soil feels very soft, but is hidden from view by the thick-bladed grass that
drapes over the terrain like a blanket, rising and falling with the rhythm of
the landscape.
name: Soft Plains

desc:
Here the tributary changes its course, heading towards the east again. It picks
up speed as it nears its destination, the Anduin. The riverbank immediately
south of here raises slightly, shielding the distant view in that direction.
name: Tributary from the Misty Mountains

desc:
The tributary is now at its fastest pace since the beginning of its journey from
the Misty Mountains. The path is blocked by a hill towards the south, so it
continues eastward, where it crashes into the rushing Anduin, causing water to
be sprayed in all directions.
name: A Swift Tributary

desc:
As the tributary continues along its path to meet the Great River, it makes a
sharp turn southwards. The eastern bank is quite grassy, and appears to be
easily accessible. The clear water is quite noisy as it rushes over and around
various large boulders that have come to rest in the path of the tributary.
name: Sharp Bend in the Tributary

desc:
The Great River continues its seemingly endless journey southwards. It is
beginning to pick up pace, and it would take great strength to swim across here.
The banks on the western side are low, and covered in lush green grass. The
water seems to get louder towards the south.
name: Bend in the Anduin River

desc:
The fury of the Anduin is evident in the chaotic maelstrom, which throws water
all over the sands that make up the eastern bank and the walls of the cliff to
the south. The glittering pebbles lying on the eastern beach seem quite
complacent as the water crashes over them and ebbs back into the river before
continuing to the west.
name: Sharp Turn in the Great River

desc:
The fast flowing waters are now completely mixed into one body and are
compressed into this gorge. Jagged walls rise directly on both sides. The water
flows extremely fast through this pass, violently continuing to the south.
name: The Great River

desc:
The Anduin flows majestically towards the south. The current is moderate and
some small eddies are gurgling near the banks. To the east the riverside is
rocky and covered with vegetation. To the west the width of the river makes it
difficult to distinguish the shape of the terrain. Some leaves and branches are
floating on the water and the sparkling shape of a fish is now and then visible
under the water's surface.
name: The Great River Anduin

desc:
Here the fast flowing tributary from the west meets the Anduin in a whirlwind of
confusion and chaos. The waters from the tributary crash upon those of the
Anduin, making swimming or boating extremely dangerous. The rocky bank towards
the east is the only way to exit the river here, if it can be reached amid the
white caps and swirling waters of this intersection.
name: Meeting of a Tributary and the Anduin

desc:
The Anduin picks up speed as it races through this gorge, flowing faster and
more violently than it had been towards the east. It is impossible to exit the
river here, as the banks are steep and unforgiving. Sharply turning towards the
south, the river carries on.
name: Fast Bend in the Anduin River

desc:
Bones of small animals and tufts of brown fur litter the ground of this cavern.
This must have once been the resting place of a big mammal or two - perhaps a
bear. The air has a musty odour and all noises seem to absorb into the brown
dirt that makes up the walls of the cavern, lending to its quiet, eerie aura.
name: Under the Hill

desc:
Various types of grasses carpet this large field, contributing to make a very
textured landscape. The ground is mostly covered by short, light green grass
which has thin, wide blades. Among the short grass grow numerous patches of
darker grass, with thin stems and a split top.
name: Field of Mixed Grasses

desc:
Most of this plain is covered in a hard, brown topsoil with many small rocks
thrown about the area. However inhospitable the soil appears to be, there are a
few thin patches of short grass that have sprouted up, and seem to be doing
quite well enduring the environment of this plain.
name: Stunted Field

desc:
An assortment of rocks and stones litter the ground here. They vary in shape,
size, texture, and even colour. One of the larger rocks in this field has a
smooth, flattened top that looks like a table. Strewn around it are many rough
stones that catch the eye because of their very dark complexion.
name: On Rocky Ground

desc:
This natural, moist cavern appears to have been formed from years of rain and
winds eating away the rock in which it resides. Many different types of rock are
jutting out of the walls of this cavern, limestone and chalk being the most
prevalent. The odd shapes and small nooks create deep shadows to the walls of
the cave.
name: Small Cavern

desc:
Large and small, almost perfectly round boulders are lying on the rocky ground.
The landscape looks rather monotonous, with a countless number of grey rocks,
indistinguishable from one another. This place has a striking lack of
vegetation, literally nothing grows on this field. A few of the boulders are
quite large, and have been cracked in half.
name: Boulder Field

desc:
Only a few speckles of green grass grow on this low, flat field. The majority of
the ground here is quite rocky, and is covered with moss of various colours,
from dark red to pale grey. The tributary from the Misty Mountains can be seen
to the north, meandering through the flatlands.
name: Barren Field

desc:
The land seems to have merely a thin layer of topsoil on a rocky surface. The
landscape is void of any tree cover, only patches of low-growing grass and
heather endure here. Some huge boulders stand on the field. This place has a
sombre feel to it, even the grass is lustreless and dull yellow in colour.
name: Barren Plain

desc:
The tributary from the Misty Mountains is quite wide here, but shallow enough to
be crossed by wading. Numerous animal tracks can be seen on the sandy riverbank,
apparently deer and other beasts favour this place to ford the river as well.
Patches of tall reeds grow by the water's edge, gently swaying in the water.
name: Ford on a Tributary

desc:
This flat waste is littered thickly with stones of different sizes, from small
pebbles to the size of a man's fist. Amidst the moss-covered stones, some silver
grey thistles with purple flowers and a few other weeds grow from the dry soil.
To the south, the tributary looks shallow enough to be crossed.
name: Rocky Flatlands

desc:
The terrain here changes very little in elevation and seems almost featureless.
Besides patches of grass and a few low-growing plants, there is not much
vegetation in this vast expanse of plains. The soil here is barren and arid,
consisting of coarse sand and rounded white stones.
name: Flatlands

desc:
The landscape is bare of shrubbery and trees, but is covered with wild grass.
The grass is waist-high and yellow in colour, for the sun has burned the tops of
the long blades. Beneath the tall yellow grass, grows numerous wild flowers and
tiny white flowers on long wiry stems.
name: Open Field

desc:
The moss-covered ground on this plain is soggy with water, with tufts of yellow
grass scattered here and there. There is little vegetation here, only a few
clumps of grey-stemmed bushes grow on the surrounding bleak and desolate expanse
of plain. The ground slopes slightly down towards the north, leading to a small
glen.
name: Damp Plain

desc:
A clump of slender birches grow on a low-lying grassy meadow here, bordered by a
tributary from the Misty Mountains. Amongst a few moss-covered rocks scattered
on the meadow, numerous brilliantly coloured wild flowers blossom on the rich
and fertile soil here. A dense growth of reeds can be seen close to the river,
rustling and swaying slowly.
name: Corner Meadow

desc:
A thick sea of tall grass, with clumps of bushes among it covers this gently
rolling plain. The blades of grass reach almost to the height of a man's chest,
making travelling wearisome. A few spider webs can be seen in the grass,
glistening with dew drops. Tall hogweeds with pungent smelling inflorescence
tower over the grass, swaying gently in the wind.
name: Sea of Tall Grass

desc:
On this riverside meadow grows dense bushes and small trees. Among the bushes
there are little open spaces carpeted with long grass. A variety of flowers
thrive here, prominantly marigolds with bright yellow flowers. The river is
almost concealed by thick vegetation, only glimpses of water can be seen in the
south and west.
name: Riverside Meadow

desc:
Thick, dark green grass thrives on this slightly undulating terrain. Tall
hogweeds rise high above the tops of the blades of grass, standing almost ten
feet high. The landscape here is mostly green, beside the white flowers of the
hogweeds, there is not much variation of colour.
name: Grassy Field

desc:
Tall grass dominates the landscape here, nourished by the tributary from the
Misty Mountains to the west and south, and the Great River Anduin on the eastern
side of the plain. The soil is extremely fertile, allowing the grass to flourish
without limit, growing to heights of eight feet in some places.
name: Thriving Grasslands

desc:
Tall grass sprinkled with colourful wild flowers grows on a thriving plain. The
terrain has very little variation in elevation and without obstructing trees or
brushwood, you get a good view of the surrounding land. Only a couple of silver
birch trees huddle together on the plains, with delicate light bark and wiry
branches.
name: Field of Flowers

desc:
The vegetation here is dominated by shrubs, ranging from low-growing, creeping
shrubs to thorny bushes of imposing height. Beneath the dull-grey spiny bushes,
heathers with bright coloured flowers flourish, varying from crimson red to
light purple. A few stumps of dead trees are here, covered with fungous, growths
of brilliant orange and greyish lichen.
name: The Brush

desc:
A twenty yards wide tributary flows through the area. The water is clear,
revealing some rocks and a sunken log at the bottom of the river. The loud noise
of rushing water can be heard from the south as the tributary apparently nears
its final destination, the Great River Anduin. The river is flowing more and
more rapidly, making it somewhat difficult to avoid getting dragged away by the
current.
name: Tributary Approaching the River Anduin

desc:
This tributary, which originated in the Misty Mountains, begins to pick up
speed, as it flows downwards to its final destination, the Anduin. The clear,
cold water is quite noisy as it flows southeast through the plains.
name: Tributary from the Misty Mountains

desc:
The tributary from the Misty Mountains flows slowly eastwards, following the
borders of a lone small hill to the south. The barren riverbanks are flanked
with large dark-grey rocks, many of them covered with green moss above the
waterline. Here and there the river flows over submerged rocks, whipping the
water into froth.
name: Tributary

desc:
The tributary takes a turn to the south here, the water flowing more rapidly as
it nears the Great River Anduin. Water rushes noisily around the large rocks in
the river, which have caught a heap of branches and twigs floating downstream.
The water flowing down from the Misty Mountains is cool and clear, enabling one
to distinguish almost every pebble that lies at the bottom.
name: Bend in a Tributary

desc:
Chilly waters from the Misty Mountains flow gently towards the Anduin River to
the East. The water running in this tributary is clear, and dark round stones
are visible at the bottom. The terrain on the riverbanks seems barren and flat,
rocky grounds with very little tree cover or vegetation.
name: Tributary

desc:
The steep riverbank is sparsely vegetated with tall shrubs, tree saplings,
herbs, and patches of grass. Below, the Great River Anduin rushes past towards
the south, and the air is thick with mist from the waters crashing against the
large rocks in the riverbank. To the west the terrain looks flat while to the
north it is elevating upwards.
name: Rocky Riverbank

desc:
A small crag rises from the grasslands, ending rather abruptly to the north and
east. The rise is covered with grass and moss-blackened granite rocks
half-buried in the ground, and a dense stand of fir trees stand together between
the rocks and shorter vegetation. The sound of the rushing waters of the river
Anduin can be heard clearly beyond the rise to the east.
name: Small Crag Overlooking the River

desc:
The walls and low-lying ceiling of the den are crossed by vein-like roots, and
an earthy odour hangs heavy in the air. The ground of this tiny chamber is lined
with dry leaves, twigs and moss, as some creature has clearly made an effort to
make the place comfortable.
name: Small Den

desc:
The ground in this secluded glen is muddy and damp. Several pools of standing
water are here, covered with rushes, and reeds. Clumps of trees dot the
landscape here and there, with rank grass and tall ferns growing beneath the
trees. The air here is foetid, with dense and moist mist hanging over the
ground.
name: Misty Glen

desc:
A ruined stone cottage is here, covered with green moss. Only two walls of the
single room cottage are still standing, providing some shelter from the
elements. In the corner of the cottage, there are scattered ashes of a camp fire
and a few charred pieces of wood. Beside the cottage grows a lone elm tree, tall
and old.
name: Ruined Cottage

desc:
This plain is barren and desolate, only patches of dry, yellow grass grow on the
arid rocky soil. The few trees here are grey and dead, with branches like giant
skeletal hands reaching for the sky. The ground is dotted with jagged granite
boulders, covered with yellowish-green lichen.
name: Barren Plain

desc:
Many shades of ochre colour the landscape, which is covered with dry grass. The
arid soil makes the terrain look lifeless and dull compared to the surrounding
thriving meadows. A few boulders are scattered about, jutting from the grass. A
withered old tree stands in the field, with a couple of limbs protruding.
name: Withered, Dry Grasslands

desc:
The grass grows tall on the plains, providing shelter and habitat for many
species of animals. A few slender birches stand amidst grass, shading a small,
natural spring of soft and pleasant water. Wild flowers grow plentiful in this
meadow, adding splashes of colour to the landscape.
name: Tall Grass

desc:
The gently rolling plains are dotted with thick bushes and few trees. The bushes
grow tall on the rich and loamy soil here, some are almost six feet tall. The
bushes have wiry, dull-green stems covered in profuse golden-yellow flowers. The
growth becomes so closely intertwined towards the south, that it seems
impossible to force a way through it.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
An abundance of flowers, grass and plants thrive on this open meadow ground. A
myriad of wild flowers blossom here, adding splashes of colour among the tall
grass. The ground declines towards the east, where a thick clump of trees and
shrubs rise from the middle of the meadow, providing some shade and hindering
travel in that direction.
name: Wild Meadow

desc:
Some large boulders, round and covered with moss, litter the grassy grounds
here. A few of the boulders are almost as tall as a man, most are somewhat
smaller. The sides of the boulders are covered with dull grey lichen, and lush,
dark green moss grows on the tops. Tussocks of thick grass flourish amidst the
rocky ground.
name: Rocky Plain

desc:
A solitary oak stands tall here, surrounded by thriving meadow grass. The grass
on this meadow is thick and hardy, growing abundantly on the loamy soil.
Numerous brightly coloured wild flowers and some tall, oblong-leaved thistles
with violet flower heads dot the mostly flat landscape.
name: Grassy Meadow

desc:
Tiny trees are sprouting up among the grasses of the meadow. The young trees are
spread out wide apart, just beginning to extend over the lengthy grass that
covers the meadow. Along the edge of the riverbank to the eastern side of the
meadow, iris plants stand out, bundled together in random locations. Each blade
of grass in the meadow is very tall, appearing both soft and refined. To the
south, a slight shift begins to form as the meadow gradually slopes down towards
the Great River.
name: Wild Meadow

desc:
The long, wild grass comes to an end here in the meadow, as it is cornered by
riverbanks on both its east and south edge. Along the riverbanks, there is a
steep decline. The soil, along those edges, is not covered by any vegetation.
Instead many tiny pebbles are embedded in the soil. The majority of the meadow,
though, is filled with thickly grown blades of grass in a well blended mixture
of green colours.
name: Corner of a Meadow

desc:
A narrow, deep cavern in the rock descends further downwards towards the north.
The walls of the cavern are smooth translucent yellow rock, resembling a frozen
waterfall. The air is damp, and you can see water seeping through cracks in the
cavern walls and ceiling.
name: Natural Cavern

desc:
A multitude of shining yellow stalactites hang from the ceiling in this cavern,
meeting stalagmites rising from the ground. Some of the stalagmites have taken
strange shapes, presumably where the dripping of the water has not always been
on the same spot. There are several puddles of water on the cavern floor.
name: Stalactite Cave

desc:
A small sheltered cove in the shoreline of the River Anduin is here, shaded by
tall willows, drooping branches almost touching the water. Water lilies with
bright white flowers grow amongst tall reeds in the calm waters separated from
the rushing river. A thick growth of willow bushes conceal the cove from its
surroundings, offering only a few potential passageways through the thicket.
name: Sheltered Cove

desc:
A tangle of sharp thorns and spines loom all around you, making travelling
difficult here. Even the ground is littered with fallen branches and twigs from
the spiny bushes. The bushes have narrow and reddish leaves and small yellow
flowers, adding little dabs of colour to the landscape. Thorny bushes grow all
the way to the rushing waters of the Great River Anduin to the east.
name: Tangled Bushes

desc:
A wild growth of thorny bushes rises around and well above you, so closely
intertwined, that it seems impossible to force a way through it. Sharp, almost
four-inch long spear-like thorns protrude from the stems and branches of the
bushes, easily piercing through garments of an unwary traveller.
name: Thorny Bushes

desc:
The clear water of the mighty Anduin River begins to pick up its pace here as it
flows into the south. Ripples throughout the water seem to make it very
difficult to view the bottom, though the water is clear. Reeds grow along the
eastern bank of this portion of the river; their long stems of green are
noticeable even from a distance.
name: Anduin River

desc:
The water of the Anduin is very cool in temperature, but very clear as well. The
water flows southbound, picking up speed again to a steady pace. Tall, slender
reeds are visible on the eastern bank, very close to the waters edge. The river
itself is extremely wide, from bank to bank, and it is several feet deep here. A
forest can be seen, though far off in the distance, beyond the eastern bank.
name: Anduin River

desc:
The bed in which the river lies, has a slight difference in its depth here. As
the water of the mighty Anduin rushes through the bed towards the south, it
becomes much deeper. Towards the east lies a riverbank filled with reeds, and a
few irises are visible. Along with the vegetation, the riverbank is also
noticeably steeper than it is upstream, which may also be the cause of the river
being deeper here as well. The water of the river is very chilly, and it is not
very clear.
name: Anduin River

desc:
High in the air, the upper parts of the fir trees rise dozens of yards above the
ground. The trees grow close together providing some shelter from the weather,
and enough cover to allow for hiding within the branches. On top of the trees
the crowns have intertwined together, and the long gnarled branches form a small
but solid platform. To the east of the fir trees, the mighty river Anduin flows
steadily to the south.
name: Upon the Branches of Fir Trees

desc:
Tall, slender trees extend their trunks and leafy branches into the sky above.
The bark, protecting each trunk of these trees, is a soft shade of brown,
appearing flawless through years of growth. It is not until several feet above
the base of the trees that their branches extend out around the trunks. Each
leaf, shaped like a small spear, is a mint-coloured green. An aged wagon remains
tilted upon its side in the centre of the forest. Thin, delicate strands of ivy
have grown over the top of the wagon remains. There is fine, green grass in
growth, stretching from around the wagon to the base of the tall trees that are
surrounding it. As this fine grass reaches the trees, it then diminishes among
the undergrowth of the forest.
name: Confined Clearing in the Forest
